title: Bread Rising Time Yeast Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Long Does Bread Rise with Yeast?
description: Bread dough rising calculator. Fresh yeast: ~1 hour at 75°F to double. Dried yeast slower. Free, instant results. Plan your baking times accurately.
intro: Bread rising time depends on yeast activity, dough temperature, hydration, and sugar content. This calculator estimates how long your dough needs to proof (first rise) or proof (second rise/final proof) based on temperature and yeast type. The core relationship follows an Arrhenius-type rate: yeast activity roughly doubles for every 15–17 °F (8–9 °C) increase in temperature within the optimal range of 75–95 °F (24–35 °C). Whether you're using active dry yeast, instant yeast, or fresh cake yeast, knowing your dough temperature is the single most important variable for predicting a reliable, consistent rise.
keyTakeaway: **Estimated Rise Time (min) = Base Time × 2^((Reference Temp − Actual Temp) / 15)** — e.g., at 70 °F a dough that rises in 60 min at 80 °F takes ≈114 min; at 95 °F it takes ≈35 min.
useCases: Adjusting bulk fermentation time when your kitchen is colder than 70 °F in winter
Planning a same-day bake schedule by knowing the dough will double in size within a specific time window
Converting a room-temperature recipe to a cold overnight proof in the refrigerator (38–40 °F)
Determining whether your active dry yeast is still viable before committing it to a full dough batch
Scaling a bakery production schedule so multiple loaves finish proofing at the same time
Troubleshooting a dough that didn't rise adequately by checking whether temperature or yeast quantity was the limiting factor
explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

Yeast fermentation rate follows an exponential relationship with temperature, modeled after the **Arrhenius equation** simplified for baking:

```
Estimated Rise Time (min) = Base Time × 2^((T_ref − T_actual) / Q10_halving)

Where:
  Base Time   = published rise time at the reference temperature (e.g., 60 min at 80 °F)
  T_ref       = reference dough temperature (°F) — typically 80 °F (27 °C)
  T_actual    = your actual dough temperature (°F)
  Q10_halving = temperature interval (°F) at which yeast activity halves ≈ 15 °F (8 °C)

Example:
  T_actual = 70 °F, T_ref = 80 °F, Base Time = 60 min
  Rise Time = 60 × 2^((80 − 70) / 15)
            = 60 × 2^(0.667)
            = 60 × 1.587
            ≈ 95 minutes
```

> **Yeast quantity adjustment:** Halving the amount of instant yeast roughly doubles the fermentation time. Doubling yeast cuts it roughly in half — useful for scheduling overnight cold proofs.

---

## Reference Table

| Dough Temperature | Relative Yeast Activity | Estimated Bulk Rise (1× to 2× volume, standard recipe) |
|---|---|---|
| 32–34 °F (0–1 °C) | Near zero | Yeast dormant; not recommended |
| 38–40 °F (3–4 °C) | ~5% of peak | 8–12 hours (cold retard / fridge proof) |
| 50 °F (10 °C) | ~25% of peak | 4–6 hours |
| 65 °F (18 °C) | ~60% of peak | 90–120 minutes |
| 75 °F (24 °C) | ~85% of peak | 75–90 minutes |
| 80 °F (27 °C) | 100% (reference) | 60 minutes |
| 85 °F (29 °C) | ~115% of peak | 45–55 minutes |
| 95 °F (35 °C) | ~130% of peak | 30–40 minutes |
| 104–115 °F (40–46 °C) | Rapid decline | Yeast begins to die; off-flavors |
| ≥138 °F (59 °C) | 0% | Yeast fully killed |

*Based on standard active dry or instant yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) at 2–2.25 tsp per 3 cups flour.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Cold Winter Kitchen (65 °F)
A recipe calls for a 60-minute first rise at 80 °F. Your kitchen is 65 °F.
```
Rise Time = 60 × 2^((80 − 65) / 15) = 60 × 2^1.0 = 60 × 2 = 120 minutes
```
**Plan for 2 hours** instead of 1 hour. Covering the bowl with plastic wrap and placing it over a warm (not hot) bowl of water can bring the environment closer to 80 °F.

### Case 2 — Overnight Refrigerator Proof (39 °F)
You want a slow cold proof. At 39 °F, yeast activity is roughly 5% of its 80 °F rate:
```
Rise Time ≈ 60 × 2^((80 − 39) / 15) = 60 × 2^2.73 ≈ 60 × 6.65 ≈ 399 minutes ≈ 6.6 hours
```
Practical bakers typically allow **8–16 hours in the fridge** to account for the ramp-down and ramp-up time as dough cools and then warms before baking. Cold proofing also develops more complex flavors via organic acid byproducts.

### Case 3 — Warm Proofing Box at 85 °F
Using an oven with just the light on (≈85 °F):
```
Rise Time = 60 × 2^((80 − 85) / 15) = 60 × 2^(−0.333) = 60 × 0.794 ≈ 48 minutes
```
At 85 °F the dough doubles **about 20% faster** than at 80 °F — useful when short on time but watch closely to avoid over-proofing.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Using water that is too hot to activate dry yeast.** Active dry yeast should be proofed in water between **105–115 °F (40–46 °C)**. Water above 120 °F (49 °C) begins killing yeast cells, resulting in a flat loaf with no second rise.

2. **Measuring rise by time instead of volume.** Rise time estimates assume a standard dough formula. A high-sugar dough (>10% sugar by flour weight) will ferment 20–30% slower because osmotic pressure inhibits yeast metabolism. Always aim for **1.5× to 2× volume**, not a fixed clock time.

3. **Ignoring bulk dough temperature vs. room temperature.** Kneading generates friction heat — hand-kneading can raise dough temperature by 2–4 °F; stand-mixer kneading can raise it by 4–8 °F. Always measure **dough temperature with an instant-read thermometer**, not air temperature.

4. **Conflating instant yeast and active dry yeast quantities.** Instant yeast (also called rapid-rise or bread machine yeast) is about **25% more potent** by weight than active dry yeast. Substituting 1:1 will shorten rise time significantly and may lead to over-proofing.

5. **Over-proofing because "more is better."** A dough risen beyond 2.5× volume has exhausted much of its fermentable sugars. The resulting bread will have a coarse, gummy crumb, little oven spring, and a weak crust. The poke test (indentation springs back slowly but does spring back) is a reliable real-time check.

---

- Since no internal related slugs were specified, check the full **Cocina** category for converters covering weight-to-volume flour substitutions, hydration percentages, and oven temperature conversions between Fahrenheit and Celsius.

faq.q: What is the ideal dough temperature for yeast bread?
faq.a: The sweet spot for *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (baker's yeast) is **75–80 °F (24–27 °C)** for the dough itself. At this range, yeast is highly active, produces CO₂ and ethanol steadily, and organic acid byproducts (lactic and acetic acid) develop at a pace that balances flavor without sourness. Dough above 90 °F starts producing excess acetic acid and risks over-fermentation within 30–40 minutes.

faq.q: How does sugar affect rising time?
faq.a: Sugar at low levels (1–5% of flour weight) *feeds* yeast and can speed fermentation slightly. At higher levels (8–15%, typical in brioche or sweet rolls), osmotic pressure draws water away from yeast cells, slowing their metabolism by **20–40%**. Recipes with >10% sugar often call for osmotolerant yeast strains or an increased yeast quantity to compensate.

faq.q: Can I use this calculator for sourdough starter?
faq.a: The temperature-vs-time relationship applies similarly to sourdough, but the microorganism mix is different — wild yeast (*S. cerevisiae* and *Kazachstania humilis*) plus lactic acid bacteria (LAB). Sourdough bulk fermentation typically takes **4–12 hours at 76–78 °F** depending on starter vigor (usually 20–25% inoculation rate). The Arrhenius multiplier still holds: expect bulk ferment to roughly double in time for every 15 °F drop.

faq.q: Does altitude affect bread rising time?
faq.a: Yes. At high altitude (e.g., Denver at 5,280 ft / 1,609 m), lower atmospheric pressure allows CO₂ bubbles to expand more easily, so dough rises **faster by roughly 25–50%** compared to sea level. The King Arthur Baking Company and USDA Extension resources recommend reducing yeast by up to 25% and cutting rising time by 15–25% above 3,500 ft to prevent over-proofing.

faq.q: What is the difference between active dry yeast and instant yeast in terms of rising time?
faq.a: Instant yeast (rapid-rise) has smaller, more porous granules that hydrate directly in dough without pre-proofing, and it is approximately **25% more active** by weight. A recipe calling for 2.25 tsp active dry yeast needs only about 1.75 tsp of instant yeast for the same rise time. Using the same amount of instant as active dry will cut the rise time by ~15–20%.

faq.q: How do I know if my yeast is still alive before using it?
faq.a: Proof your yeast: dissolve 1 tsp of sugar in ½ cup of water at **110 °F (43 °C)**, sprinkle 1 packet (2.25 tsp / 7 g) of active dry yeast, and wait 10 minutes. A healthy yeast culture will produce a **foamy, bubbly layer at least ½ inch (1.25 cm) thick**. Little or no foam means the yeast is expired or was killed by heat. Yeast stored in the freezer remains viable for up to 1–2 years; shelf-stable packets in a cool pantry last until the printed expiration date.

faq.q: What happens if I let dough over-proof?
faq.a: Over-proofed dough has exhausted most fermentable sugars and gluten integrity is compromised by excess CO₂ and organic acids. The bread will collapse during baking (no oven spring), produce a **dense, gummy crumb**, and have a pale, weakly-colored crust due to depleted Maillard reaction sugars. Minor over-proofing can sometimes be rescued by gently degassing and reshaping the dough, allowing a short 20–30 minute second rise before baking.

faq.q: Why does refrigerator proofing improve flavor?
faq.a: Cold fermentation (38–40 °F / 3–4 °C) slows yeast CO₂ production but allows enzymatic activity (amylases, proteases) and LAB acids to continue developing over 8–24 hours. The result is higher concentrations of **lactic acid, acetic acid, and complex esters** — contributing to a more complex, slightly tangy flavor and a more extensible gluten network. The USDA notes that slow fermentation also slightly increases the bioavailability of minerals in whole-grain doughs due to phytate breakdown.

faq.q: How much yeast should I use per cup of flour?
faq.a: A general rule for standard bread recipes: use **¼ tsp to ½ tsp of instant yeast per cup (120 g) of bread flour** for a room-temperature bulk fermentation of 1–2 hours. For an overnight cold proof, reduce to **⅛ tsp per cup** to avoid over-fermentation in the fridge. Using a full packet (2.25 tsp) for 3 cups of flour is the standard American recipe quantity, targeting a ~60-minute rise at 80 °F.
